title: Body condition scoring a sow
subtitle: Assessing a sow's fat cover and adjusting her ration accordingly.
excerpt: A sow that is too thin going into farrowing has fewer piglets and lower milk production. A sow that is too fat has difficult farrowings and more overlying of piglets. Body condition scoring by hand gives you a reliable way to adjust the ration at the four management points that matter most.
sourceNotes: AHDB pig body condition scoring guide, ahdb.org.uk. BPEX pig management manual. DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs.

glossaryTerms.definition: A numerical assessment of a sow's fat reserves, scored 1 to 5 by handling the hip bones, backbone, and ribs. Score 3 is the target at most management points.
Increasing the energy intake of a sow or gilt in the two to three weeks before service to improve conception rate and litter size.

content.text: The fat cover a sow carries at mating, through pregnancy, and going into farrowing has a direct effect on litter size, piglet birth weight, and first-week survival. The
takes five minutes. It tells you whether to adjust feed before the next key point in her cycle.
Score 1: Very thin. Prominent backbone and hipbones. No fat cover detectable. Ribs visible.
Score 2: Thin. Backbone prominent but covered by a thin layer of fat. Hipbones visible and sharp to touch. Ribs easily palpable.
Score 3: Ideal. Backbone not visible but palpable with moderate pressure. Hipbones not visible; you can feel them with firm pressure. Ribs covered.
Score 4: Fat. Backbone cannot be felt even with moderate pressure. Hip dimples visible but bones rounded. Sow feels blocky.
Score 5: Obese. Cannot feel any bony prominences. Heavy, rounded appearance. Should not be bred in this condition.
When to score and what to aim for
At weaning: target score 2.5 to 3. A thin sow at weaning needs
At service: target score 3. Conception rate and litter size are both affected by condition at mating.
At weeks 4 to 6 of gestation: adjust ration to recover or maintain score 3. Mid-gestation adjustment has more effect than late-gestation changes.
At farrowing: target score 3 to 3.5. A sow going into farrowing at score 2 or below loses more body weight during lactation and comes back into oestrus later.

Approach the sow calmly and handle her alongside the pen fence if she is unfamiliar with handling. You need to press firmly over the sacral region (rump) and along the lumbar spine (back) to feel through the fat. Use the flat of the hand, not fingers, and apply gradual firm pressure. A sow that objects can be contained briefly with a pig board while you assess.

Gilts, first-time mothers, should be entering farrowing at score 3 to 3.5. A lean gilt has a harder farrowing and a more difficult lactation. Her litter will be smaller on average than a mature sow's. This is normal; gilts are not as efficient as mature sows until their second or third parity.
